title: Comparing with z-scores
description: Practice comparing the relative standing of two data points using their z-scores.
topic_path: Exploring one-variable quantitative data: Percentiles, z-scores, and the normal distribution

assessment_items.question_content:
Before applying to law school in the US, students need to take an exam called the LSAT. Before applying to medical school, students need to take an exam called the MCAT. Here are some summary statistics for each exam:

Exam | Mean | Standard deviation
- | - | -
LSAT | $\mu=151$ | $\sigma=10$
MCAT | $\mu=25.1$ | $\sigma=6.4$

Juwan took both exams. He scored $172$ on the LSAT and $37$ on the MCAT.

**On which test did Juwan score relatively better, compared to others who took the test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above the mean Juwan's scores were on each test.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{Juwan's score})-(\text{mean score})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Juwan's $z$-score on the LSAT:**

Juwan's score: $\blueD{172}$  
LSAT mean: $\greenD{151}$  
LSAT standard deviation: $\purpleD{10}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{LSAT}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{172}-\greenD{151}}{\purpleD{10}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{21}{10}\\\\
&=2.1\end{align}$

**Calculating Juwan's $z$-score on the MCAT:**

Juwan's score: $\blueD{37}$  
MCAT mean: $\greenD{25.1}$  
MCAT standard deviation: $\purpleD{6.4}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{MCAT}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{37}-\greenD{25.1}}{\purpleD{6.4}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{11.9}{6.4}\\\\
&\approx1.86
\end{align}$

**Did Juwan score relatively higher on the LSAT or the MCAT?**

Juwan's $z$-score was higher on the LSAT, so he was relatively farther above average on that exam than he was on the MCAT.

He was more than $2$ standard deviations above the mean on the LSAT $(z=2.1)$, but he was a little less than $2$ standard deviations above the mean on the MCAT $(z\approx1.86)$.
Juwan scored better on the LSAT relative to others who took that test.

choices.content: Juwan scored better on the LSAT relative to others who took that test.
Juwan did better on the MCAT relative to others who took that test.
Juwan scored equally well relative to others on both tests.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual test scores.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Appalachian Trail is a long hiking trail that takes several months to travel by foot. Hikers sometimes change sleeping bags during the trip because of changing seasons. 

Each sleeping bag has a rating telling the minimum outside temperature in which it is effective. Here are some summary statistics for the ratings for the sleeping bags hikers were using at the beginning and end of their trip:

Time | Mean rating | Standard deviation
- | - | -
Beginning | $\mu=21^{\circ}\text{F}$ | $\sigma=11^{\circ}\text{F}$
End | $\mu=32^{\circ}\text{F}$ | $\sigma=12.9^{\circ}\text{F}$

Irina used a sleeping bag rated $15^{\circ}\text{F}$ at the beginning of the trip and a sleeping bag rated $29^{\circ}\text{F}$ at the end of her trip on the Appalachian Trail.

[https://thetrek.co/top-sleeping-bags-appalachian-trail-2015/]: (source) 

**Relative to other sleeping bags hikers were using at the time, which of Irina's sleeping bags had a relatively lower rating?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations below the mean each sleeping bag's rating was. 

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{sleeping bag rating})-(\text{mean rating})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Irina's sleeping bag $z$-score at the beginning of the trip:**

Sleeping bag rating: $\blueD{15}$  
Beginning of the trip mean: $\greenD{21}$  
Beginning of the trip standard deviation: $\purpleD{11}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{beginning}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{15}-\greenD{21}}{\purpleD{11}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-6}{11}\\\\
&=-0.\overline{54}
\end{align}$

**Calculating Irina's sleeping bag $z$-score at the end of the trip:**

Sleeping bag rating: $\blueD{29}$  
End of the trip mean: $\greenD{32}$  
End of the trip standard deviation: $\purpleD{12.9}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{end}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{29}-\greenD{32}}{\purpleD{12.9}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{-3}{12.9} \\\\
&\approx -0.23\end{align}$

**Did Irina's sleeping bag have a relatively  lower rating at the beginning or end of her trip?**

The sleeping bag $z$-score was lower at the beginning of the trip, so it was farther below average at that time than at the end of the trip.

It was more than $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a standard deviation below the mean at the beginning $(z=-0.\overline{54})$, but it was less than $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a standard deviation below the mean at the end $(z\approx -0.23)$.
Irina's sleeping bag at the beginning of her trip had a lower relative rating.

choices.content: Irina's sleeping bag at the beginning of her trip had a lower relative rating.
Irina's sleeping bag at the end of her trip had a lower relative rating.
Both of Irina's sleeping bags had equal relative ratings.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual sleeping bag ratings.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Students applying to a certain military academy must take a fitness test. One component of the test has applicants complete as many sit-ups as possible in fixed amount of time. Higher numbers of sit-ups are preferred, and the academy calculates the results separately for males and females. Here are some summary statistics for the sit-ups portion of the fitness test:

Gender| Mean | Standard deviation
- | - | -
Male | $\mu=81$ | $\sigma=4.7$
Female | $\mu=78$ | $\sigma=5.7$

Vitor (who is male) and Manuela (who is female) both completed $80$ sit-ups on the fitness test.

[http://www.academyadmissions.com/admissions/the-application-process/fitness-assessment/]: (Source - could use this to swap to 2 events, instead of 2 genders for same event.)

**Relative to their gender, who completed more sit-ups?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above or below the mean each person's result was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{person's result})-(\text{mean result})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Vitor's sit-up $z$-score:**

Vitor's sit-ups: $\blueD{80}$  
Male mean: $\greenD{81}$  
Male standard deviation: $\purpleD{4.7}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Vitor}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{80}-\greenD{81}}{\purpleD{4.7}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-1}{4.7} \\\\
&\approx -0.21
\end{align}$

**Calculating Manuela's sit-up $z$-score:**

Manuela's sit-ups: $\blueD{80}$  
Female mean: $\greenD{78}$  
Female standard deviation: $\purpleD{5.7}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Manuela}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{80}-\greenD{78}}{\purpleD{5.7}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{5.7} \\\\
&\approx 0.35
\end{align}$

**Did Vitor or Manuela have the relatively higher result?**

Manuela had a higher $z$-score, so she was farther above average on the sit-up portion of the fitness test.

Her result was above the mean $(z\approx 0.35)$, but Vitor's result was below the mean $(z\approx -0.21)$.
Manuela completed more sit-ups relative to her gender.

choices.content: Vitor
Manuela
Vitor and Manuela completed an equal number of sit-ups relative to their genders.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual results.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers in an early childhood development organization  investigated the number of words in the vocabularies of young children. Here are some summary statistics:

Age | Mean | Standard deviation
- | - | -
$18$ months | $\mu=85$ words | $\sigma=80$ words
$24$ months | $\mu=275$ words | $\sigma=120$ words

Miku (who is $18$ months old) has a vocabulary of $93$ words, while Tessa (who is $24$ months old) has a vocabulary of $287$ words.

[http://www.ling.upenn.edu/courses/ling001/acquisition.html]: (Source, but I changed 122 to 124)

**Relative to their age, who has a larger vocabulary?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above the mean each child's vocabulary size is.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{vocabulary size})-(\text{mean vocabulary size})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Miku's $z$-score:**

Miku's vocabulary size: $\blueD{93}$  
$18$ months mean: $\greenD{85}$  
$18$ months standard deviation: $\purpleD{80}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Miku}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{93}-\greenD{85}}{\purpleD{80}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{8}{80} \\\\
&\approx 0.1
\end{align}$

**Calculating Tessa's $z$-score:**

Tessa's vocabulary size: $\blueD{287}$  
$24$ months mean: $\greenD{275}$  
$24$ months standard deviation: $\purpleD{120}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Tessa}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{287}-\greenD{275}}{\purpleD{120}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{12}{120} \\\\
&\approx 0.1
\end{align}$

**Does Mika or Tessa have the relatively larger vocabulary?**

Mika and Tessa have the equal $z$-scores, so their vocabulary sizes are equal relative to their ages.

Both vocabulary sizes were about $\dfrac{1}{10}$ of a standard deviation above the mean $(z\approx 0.1)$.
Miku and Tessa have equally sized vocabularies relative to their ages.

choices.content: Miku
Tessa
Miku and Tessa have equally sized vocabularies relative to their ages.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual vocabulary sizes.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Many investment decisions depend on a stock's performance relative to its own history. Here are some summary statistics about the closing prices of two stocks over the past year:

Stock | Mean | Standard deviation
- | - | -
Stock $A$ | $\mu=\$4$ | $\sigma=\$0.50$ 
Stock $B$ | $\mu=\$32$ | $\sigma=\$5.75$ 

Today, Eduardo bought shares at their closing prices in stock $A$ for $\$4.75$ each, while he bought shares in stock $B$ for $\$28.50$ each.

[http://www.ling.upenn.edu/courses/ling001/acquisition.html]: (Source)

**For which stock did Eduardo pay less, relative to its previous prices?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above or below the mean each stock's price was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{price})-(\text{mean closing price})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating stock $A$'s $z$-score:**

Stock $A$'s price: $\$\blueD{4.75}$  
Stock $A$'s annual mean: $\$\greenD{4}$  
Stock $A$'s annual standard deviation: $\$\purpleD{0.50}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{A}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{4.75}-\greenD{4}}{\purpleD{0.5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{0.75}{0.5} \\\\
&=1.5
\end{align}$

**Calculating stock $B$'s $z$-score:**

Stock $B$'s price: $\$\blueD{28.50}$  
Stock $B$'s annual mean: $\$\greenD{32}$  
Stock $B$'s annual standard deviation: $\$\purpleD{5.75}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{B}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{28.50}-\greenD{32}}{\purpleD{5.75}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-3.5}{5.75} \\\\
&\approx -0.61
\end{align}$

**Did Eduardo pay a relatively better price for stock $A$ or stock $B$?**

Stock $B$ had the lower $z$-score, so its price was farther below the average.

Stock $B$'s price today was below the mean $(z\approx -0.61)$, but stock $A$'s price was above the mean $(z=1.5)$.
Eduardo paid a relatively lower price for stock $B$.

choices.content: Stock $A$
Stock $B$
Eduardo paid an equal amount for each stock relative to their previous prices.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual prices.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
A town hosted two races—a full marathon and a half marathon—and recorded the finishing times for both races. Here are some summary statistics:


Race | Mean | Standard deviation
- | - | -
Full | $\mu=285\,\text{min}$ | $\sigma=50\,\text{min}$
Half | $\mu=140\,\text{min}$ | $\sigma=20\,\text{min}$

Rex ran the full marathon in $185$ minutes, and his friend Lisa ran the half marathon in $100$ minutes.

**Relative to their race, who finished with a faster time?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations below the mean each runner's time was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{runner's time})-(\text{mean time})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Rex's z-score in the full marathon:**

Rex's time: $\blueD{185}\,\text{min}$  
Full marathon mean: $\greenD{285}\,\text{min}$  
Full marathon standard deviation: $\purpleD{50}\,\text{min}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Rex}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{185}-\greenD{285}}{\purpleD{50}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-100}{50}\\\\
&=-2
\end{align}$

**Calculating Lisa's z-score in the half marathon:**

Lisa's time: $\blueD{100}\,\text{min}$  
Half marathon mean: $\greenD{140}\,\text{min}$  
Half marathon standard deviation: $\purpleD{20}\,\text{min}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Lisa}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{100}-\greenD{140}}{\purpleD{20}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-40}{20}\\\\
&=-2
\end{align}$

**Who had a faster finishing time?** 

Both runners were the same distance from average in their race.

Rex was $2$ standard deviations below the mean in his race $(z=-2)$, and so was Lisa in her race $(z=-2)$.
Rex and Lisa finished with equally fast times relative to their races.

choices.content: Rex
Lisa
Rex and Lisa finished with equally fast times relative to their races
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual finishing times.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Female barn owls tend to be larger than male barn owls. Here are some summary statistics for the masses of the barn owls sampled from one region.

Gender| Mean | Standard deviation
- | - | -
Male | $\mu=15.75\,\text{oz}$ | $\sigma=2.5\,\text{oz}$
Female | $\mu=17.5\,\text{oz}$ | $\sigma=3\,\text{oz}$

A male and a female barn owl, each with a mass of $14\,\text{oz}$, live on Aya's property.

**Relative to its gender, which owl has a smaller mass?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations below the mean each owl's mass is.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{owl's mass})-(\text{mean mass})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating the male owl's $z$-score:**

Male barn owl's mass: $\blueD{14}$  
Male mean: $\greenD{15.75}$  
Male standard deviation: $\purpleD{2.5}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{male}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{14}-\greenD{15.75}}{\purpleD{2.5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-1.75}{2.5} \\\\
&= -0.7
\end{align}$

**Calculating the female owl's $z$-score:**

Female barn owl's mass: $\blueD{14}$  
Female mean: $\greenD{17.5}$  
Female standard deviation: $\purpleD{3}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{female}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{14}-\greenD{17.5}}{\purpleD{3}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-3.5}{3} \\\\
&= -1.1\overline{6}
\end{align}$

**Relative to its gender, which owl has a smaller mass?**

The female barn owl had the lower $z$-score, so her mass was farther below the average for other female barn owls in the region.

The female's mass was more than $1$ standard deviation below the mean $(z=-1.1\overline{6})$, but the male's mass was less than $1$ standard deviation below the mean $(z=-0.7)$.
The female owl has a smaller mass relative to its gender.

choices.content: The male
The female
The male and female have an equal mass relative to their genders.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual masses.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
People tend to be members in the Senate later in their careers than they are in the House of Representatives. Here are some summary statistics for the ages of members of the House of Representatives and the Senate one year in the United States.

Group | Mean age | Standard deviation
- | - | -
Senate | $\mu=62$ years| $\sigma=10$ years
House of Representatives| $\mu=58$ years | $\sigma=11$ years

The Senate minority leader that year was $66$ years old, while the House of Representatives minority leader was $76$ years old.

**Relative to their group, which leader was older?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above the mean each leader's age was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{leader's age})-(\text{mean age})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating the Senate minority leader's $z$-score:**

Senate minority leader's age: $\blueD{66}$  
Senate mean: $\greenD{62}$  
Senate standard deviation: $\purpleD{10}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Senate}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{66}-\greenD{62}}{\purpleD{10}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{4}{10} \\\\
&= 0.4
\end{align}$

**Calculating the House of Representatives minority leader's $z$-score:**

House of Representatives minority leader's age: $\blueD{76}$  
House of Representatives mean: $\greenD{58}$  
House of Representatives standard deviation: $\purpleD{11}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{House}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{76}-\greenD{58}}{\purpleD{11}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{18}{11} \\\\
&= 1.\overline{63}
\end{align}$

**Which leader was relatively older?**

The House of Representatives minority leader had the higher $z$-score, so her age was farther above the average.

The House of Representatives minority leader's age was more than $1$ standard deviation above the mean $(z=1.\overline{63})$, but the Senate minority leader's age was less than $1$ standard deviation above the mean $(z=0.4)$.
The House of Representatives minority leader was older relative to their group.

choices.content: The Senate minority leader
The House of Representatives minority leader
The leaders had equal ages relative to their groups.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual ages.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here are some summary statistics for the ages of students in the undergraduate and graduate programs at one university.

Degree level | Mean age | Standard deviation
- | - | -
Undergraduate| $\mu=22$ years| $\sigma=5$ years
Graduate | $\mu=38$ years | $\sigma=12.5$ years

Isabella is a $20$-year-old undergraduate student and Hannah is a $33$-year-old graduate student at the university.

**Relative to their degree level, which student is younger?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations below the mean each student's age was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{student's age})-(\text{mean age})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Isabella's age $z$-score:**

Isabella's age: $\blueD{20}$  
Undergraduate mean: $\greenD{22}$  
Undergraduate standard deviation: $\purpleD{5}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Isabella}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{20}-\greenD{22}}{\purpleD{5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-2}{5} \\\\
&= -0.4
\end{align}$

**Calculating Hannah's age $z$-score:**

Hannah's age: $\blueD{33}$  
Graduate mean: $\greenD{38}$  
Graduate standard deviation: $\purpleD{12.5}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Hannah}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{33}-\greenD{38}}{\purpleD{12.5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-5}{12.5} \\\\
&= -0.4
\end{align}$

**Is Isabella or Hannah relatively younger?**

Both students were the same distance from the average for their degree levels.

Isabella was a little less than half a standard deviation below the average $(z=-0.4)$ for her degree level, and so was Hannah in her degree level $(z=-0.4)$.
Isabella and Hannah are equally young relative to their groups.

choices.content: Isabella
Hannah
Isabella and Hannah are equally young relative to their groups.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual ages.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Private colleges tend to cost more than public colleges. Here are some summary statistics for the amount of college debt that undergraduate students graduate with from $4$-year institutions in one region.

Institution type | Mean debt (thousands of dollars) | Standard deviation (thousands of dollars)
- | - | -
Private| $\mu=\$24$ | $\sigma=\$16$ 
Public| $\mu=\$18$ | $\sigma=\$15$ 

Niklas graduated from a private institution and Cem graduated from a public institution. Each had $\$35$ thousand dollars of college debt.

[http://www.finaid.org/educators/20100929debtdistribution.pdf]: (Derived from source)

**Relative to their institution type, who graduated with more debt?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above or below the mean each student's debt was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{student's debt})-(\text{mean debt})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Niklas's debt $z$-score:**

Niklas's debt: $\blueD{35}$  
Private mean: $\greenD{24}$  
Private standard deviation: $\purpleD{16}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Niklas}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{35}-\greenD{24}}{\purpleD{16}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{11}{16} \\\\
&= 0.6875
\end{align}$

**Calculating Cem's debt $z$-score:**

Cem's debt: $\blueD{35}$  
Public mean: $\greenD{18}$  
Public standard deviation: $\purpleD{15}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Cem}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{35}-\greenD{18}}{\purpleD{15}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{17}{15} \\\\
&= 1.1\overline{3}
\end{align}$

**Did Niklas or Cem graduate with relatively more debt?**

Cem had the higher $z$-score, so his debt was farther above the average.

Niklas was less than $1$ standard deviation above the average $(z=0.6875)$ for graduating from a private institution, but Cem was more than $1$ standard deviation above the mean $z=1.1\overline{3}$ for graduating from a public institution.
Cem graduated with more debt relative to his institution type.

choices.content: Niklas
Cem
Niklas and Cem graduated with equally as much debt relative to their institution types.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual debt amounts.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here are some summary statistics for the durations for which candles from a certain company burn.

Candle type | Mean duration | Standard deviation 
- | - | -
Jar candle | $\mu=25$ hours | $\sigma=2.5$ hours 
Pillar candle | $\mu=95$ hours | $\sigma=7.5$ hours 

Andrei had cinnamon-scented jar candle that burned for $30$ hours and a berry-scented pillar candle that burned for $88$ hours.

**Relative to its type, which candle burned faster?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above or below the mean each candle's duration was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{candle's duration})-(\text{mean duration})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating the cinnamon-scented candle's duration $z$-score:**

Cinnamon-scented candle's duration: $\blueD{30}$  
Jar candle mean: $\greenD{25}$  
Jar candle standard deviation: $\purpleD{2.5}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{cinnamon}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{30}-\greenD{25}}{\purpleD{2.5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{2.5} \\\\
&= 2
\end{align}$

**Calculating the berry-scented candle's duration $z$-score:**

Berry-scented candle's duration: $\blueD{88}$  
Pillar candle mean: $\greenD{95}$  
Pillar candle standard deviation: $\purpleD{7.5}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{berry}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{88}-\greenD{95}}{\purpleD{7.5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-7}{7.5} \\\\
&= -0.9\overline{3}
\end{align}$

**Did cinnamon-scented or berry-scented candle burn relatively faster?**

The berry-scented candle had the lower $z$-score, so its duration was farther below average.

The berry-scented candle's duration was below the average for pillar candles $(z=-0.9\overline{3})$, but the cinnamon-scented candle's duration was above the average for jar candles $(z=2)$.
The berry-scented pillar candle burned faster relative to its type.

choices.content: The cinnamon-scented jar candle
The berry-scented pillar candle
The two candles burned equally fast relative to their types.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual burning times.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here are some summary statistics for the life spans of two breeds of cats.

Breed | Mean life span| Standard deviation 
- | - | -
Abyssinian | $\mu=12$ years| $\sigma=1.5$ years
Colorpoint shorthair | $\mu=14$ years | $\sigma=1$ year

Fluffy was an Abyssinian cat who lived for $13$ years, and Mittens was a colorpoint shorthair cat who lived for $15$ years.

[https://www.petcarerx.com/article/the-average-lifespan-of-a-cat-breed-by-breed-chart/1698]: (Source - used printed range as containing 95% of the population.)

**Relative to its breed, which cat had a longer lifespan?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above the mean each cat's lifespan was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{cat's lifespan})-(\text{mean lifespan})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating Fluffy's $z$-score:**

Fluffy's lifespan: $\blueD{13}$  
Abyssinian mean: $\greenD{12}$  
Abyssinian standard deviation: $\purpleD{1.5}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Fluffy}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{13}-\greenD{12}}{\purpleD{1.5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{1.5} \\\\
&=0.\overline{6}
\end{align}$

**Calculating Mittens's $z$-score:**

Mittens's lifespan: $\blueD{15}$  
Colorpoint shorthair mean: $\greenD{14}$  
Colorpoint shorthair standard deviation: $\purpleD{1}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Mittens}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{15}-\greenD{14}}{\purpleD{1}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{1} \\\\
&=1
\end{align}$

**Did Fluffy or Mittens have a relatively longer lifespan?**

Mittens had the greater $z$-score, so her lifespan was farther above the average.

Mittens's life span was $1$ standard deviation above the mean for her breed $(z=1)$, but Fluffy's life span was less than $1$ standard deviation above the mean for his breed $(z=0.\overline{6})$.
Mittens had a longer lifespan relative to their breed.

choices.content: Fluffy
Mittens
Fluffy and Mittens had equally long lifespans relative to their breeds.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual lifespans.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here are some summary statistics for the percentage of salt content for two seasoning blends a company produces.

Blend| Mean salt content | Standard deviation 
- | - | -
Seasoning salt| $\mu=56\%$ | $\sigma=3\%$ 
Cajun seasoning | $\mu=46\%$ | $\sigma=2\%$ 

Kaylee bought a bottle of seasoning salt that contained $59\%$ salt and a bottle of Cajun seasoning that contained $44\%$ salt.

**Relative to the type of blend, which bottle had a lower salt content?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above or below the mean each bottle's salt content was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{bottle's salt content})-(\text{mean salt content})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating the seasoning salt's $z$-score:**

Kaylee's seasoning salt's salt content: $\blueD{59\%}$  
Seasoning salt mean: $\greenD{56\%}$  
Seasoning salt standard deviation: $\purpleD{3\%}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{seasoning salt}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{59}-\greenD{56}}{\purpleD{3}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{3} \\\\
&=1
\end{align}$

**Calculating the Cajun seasoning's $z$-score:**

Kaylee's Cajun seasoning's salt content: $\blueD{44\%}$  
Cajun seasoning's mean: $\greenD{46\%}$  
Cajun seasoning's standard deviation: $\purpleD{2\%}$

$\begin{align}z_{\text{Cajun seasoning}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{44}-\greenD{46}}{\purpleD{2}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-2}{2} \\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

**Did the seasoning salt or the Cajun seasoning that Kaylee bought have the relatively lower salt content?**

The Cajun seasoning's $z$-score is lower, so it's salt content is farther below the average.

The Cajun seasoning Kaylee bought is $1$ standard deviation below the average salt content for its blend, while the seasoning salt is $1$ standard deviation above the average salt content for its blend.
Kaylee's bottle of Cajun seasoning had a lower salt content relative to its blend.

choices.content: Kaylee's bottle of seasoning salt
Kaylee's bottle of Cajun seasoning
Both of her bottles had equal salt content relative to their blends.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual salt contents.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here are some summary statistics for the numbers of acres of soybeans and peanuts harvested per county in Alabama in 2009, for counties that planted those crops.

Crop| Mean harvest (thousands of acres) | Standard deviation  (thousands of acres)
- | - | -
Soybeans | $\mu=12$ | $\sigma=14$ 
Peanuts | $\mu=10$ | $\sigma=8$ 

In one southern county, there were $9$ thousand acres of soybeans harvested and $3$ thousand acres of peanuts harvested.

**Relative to its crop, which plant had a better harvest?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations below the mean each plant's harvest was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{plant's harvest})-(\text{mean harvest})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating the county's soybean harvest $z$-score:**

County's soybean harvest: $\blueD{9} $ thousand acres  
Soybean mean: $\greenD{12}$ thousand acres  
Soybean standard deviation: $\purpleD{14}$ thousand acres

$\begin{align}z_{\text{soybean}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{9}-\greenD{12}}{\purpleD{14}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-3}{14} \\\\
&\approx-0.21
\end{align}$

**Calculating the county's peanut harvest $z$-score:**

County's peanut harvest: $\blueD{3} $ thousand acres  
Peanut mean: $\greenD{10}$ thousand acres  
Peanut standard deviation: $\purpleD{8}$ thousand acres

$\begin{align}z_{\text{peanut}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{3}-\greenD{10}}{\purpleD{8}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-7}{8} \\\\
&=-0.875
\end{align}$

**Did the county's soybeans or peanuts have a relatively better harvest?**

The soybean harvest's $z$-score is higher, so the acres harvested are not as far below the average.

The soybean harvest in the county was less than $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a standard deviation below the average $(z\approx-0.21)$, but the peanut harvest in the county was more than $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a standard deviation below the average $(z=-0.875)$.
The soybeans had a better harvest relative to their crop.

choices.content: The soybeans
The peanuts
The soybeans and peanuts had equally good harvests relative to their crops.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the harvest amounts.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Because of the wavelengths used in different kinds of radio broadcasting, AM radio signals often travel farther than FM radio signals do. Here are some summary statistics for the broadcasting ranges of radio stations in one region.

Type of station| Mean broadcast range | Standard deviation 
- | - | -
AM | $\mu=320\,\text{km}$ | $\sigma=80\,\text{km}$ 
FM | $\mu=88\,\text{km}$ | $\sigma=5\,\text{km}$ 

The KHAN radio station is an AM station with a broadcast range of $352\,\text{km}$, and the KFAL radio station is an FM station with a broadcast range of $90\,\text{km}$.

**Relative to its station type, which station broadcasts farther?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We need to calculate how many standard deviations above the mean each station's broadcast range was.

A z-score measures how many standard deviations  from the mean a data point is, and whether the data point is above or below the mean.

$z=\dfrac{(\text{station's broadcast range})-(\text{mean broadcast range})}{\text{standard deviation}}$

**Calculating the KHAN station's $z$-score:**

KHAN's broadcast range: $\blueD{352\,\text{km}} $   
AM station mean: $\greenD{320\,\text{km}}$  
AM station standard deviation: $\purpleD{80\,\text{km}}$ 

$\begin{align}z_{\text{KHAN}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{352}-\greenD{320}}{\purpleD{80}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{32}{80} \\\\
&=0.4
\end{align}$

**Calculating the KFAL station's $z$-score:**

KFAL's broadcast range: $\blueD{90\,\text{km}} $   
FM station mean: $\greenD{88\,\text{km}}$  
FM station standard deviation: $\purpleD{5\,\text{km}}$ 

$\begin{align}z_{\text{KFAL}}&=\dfrac{\blueD{90}-\greenD{88}}{\purpleD{5}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{5} \\\\
&=0.4
\end{align}$

**Does KHAN or KFAL broadcast relatively farther?**

Both stations were the same distance from the average for their kind of station.

The KHAN station was $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of a standard deviation above the mean $(z=0.4)$ for its kind of station, and so was the KFAL station.
KHAN and KFAL broadcast equally far relative to their station types.

choices.content: KHAN
KFAL
KHAN and KFAL broadcast equally far relative to their station types.
It's impossible to say without seeing all of the individual broadcast ranges.
It's impossible to say since we don't know the shape of either distribution.
